any questions about anything? Okay, so what I want to do today is finish up some general principles of remote procedure call, and I'm switching things around a bit. So then I want to talk about other styles of communication in distributed systems, and then on Wednesday come back and look very specifically at an RPC implementation that we're going to actually use in class. So that's the plan. And because I was on the board, last time I stopped a little short of what I wanted to say, so I want to pick up where I was. So just a kind of reminder of the picture. So the idea behind Remote Procedure Call is that you give a look and feel of a procedure interface that is quite familiar to programmers. except that that procedure is executing in another address space, potentially on another machine. And that, in some sense, is our model of communication. I'm going to ask you to do something for me or communicate some information to you through a procedure call-like interface. So that's a very natural way to think about programming things. And remember to create that illusion or that transparency to the client or to the user, what we need is some software running on the client machine called a client stub. And who can remind us what is the job of the client stub? Yeah? To serialize the data and send it to the back home? So it has to serialize the parameters into a buffer, because that is a contiguous buffer of data is what the network layer requires for communication. And then the sub figures out how actually to establish a connection to the server and how to actually then pass that message and what the message should look like. So it does quite a bit of heavy lifting. and then the message is then sent out across the network and again we're relying on the underlying transport layer provided by internet protocol stack. The server machine and the process on the server machine receives that message through the underlying internet communication protocol like TCP and then it has to deserialize the message to figure out what is in there because the server might support a large range of different procedures So it has to figure out what did the client want.

So it has to take apart that message, peer inside it, and see what they were looking for. And then based on what they're looking for, there's usually a large kind of if statement to figure out, okay, what procedure did they request? And given the one they request, it will then make a local call to the implementation of that procedure, the actual code that's going to do it. And so that code is going to be implemented by whoever implemented the server and carried out. And if you look at that code, generally, it's pretty much like, well, how I would do it if I were to do it myself, with maybe some slight variations depending on the type of RPC. And so then the procedure is called locally. It is then has a reply and answer to the procedure, whatever the output is. It is then repackaged, re -serialized into a message that can go back to the client stub. The client stub sends the message and is waiting for the reply, request reply, and the client therefore is waiting as well, and then that answer is de-serialized out of the message and comes back through the normal procedure call mechanism from the stub, because in the end, where the client program is calling the stub, and so it's just waiting for that answer to come back, okay? Were there any questions about the concept of this, and how the stubs get created, who writes them, are they integrated into the language? Many different ways that that can happen, but for now I just want to give you, you know, understanding of the kind of the plumbing, as we say, of how it works. OK. So this all sounds really great, assuming that somebody implements the stubs for us. And normally, somebody will. And we talked about a number of different issues that we'll look at specifically in the context of GRPC in a little bit. But one issue is how the stub can know the server. I'm just calling a procedure. How do I know where that procedure is? So the stub somehow has to be told or discover where is that service? Where is the server that can do this for me? And normally when you're programming out in the wild, you kind of know. It's like you kind of know where the service

is. Or if you don't, you could request it from another service might be able to tell you. I just want to mention that there's another sort of terminology you might hear out there. And so sometimes the connection point that the client makes to the server, sometimes that's called an endpoint, a popular term for that to be used these days. And a server may have many so-called endpoints. And the endpoints, in some sense, kind of specify which server you're talking to. And, again, within that message that you're sending to that endpoint, then the specific function is usually accessed. So the client somehow has to get the endpoint or this address of the server. I think you can think of it as an IP address and a port number, just some way to communicate at the transport layer. And then it sends that serialized message to that particular endpoint at the server. So either it knows the endpoint already, which is kind of a more common case, or it might ask another process for that endpoint. Okay. There may be some, maybe I know the server machine, but I don't know the server process on that machine. So there might be another globally known service on the client machine, some kind of daemon, where I know how to talk to it because it's globally known. And I can say, well, what's the endpoint to ask for the add service or the multiply service or whatever? And then it can return that. And again, this would be done kind of at the stub level below what the user would end up seeing. But endpoint is a term you'll hear. Quite a bit out in the practice of distributed systems. Okay. So we mentioned that the goal of the RPC is transparency, meaning that as someone we had a little exchange on Piazza. It's sort of the opposite definition of transparent in real life where you can see through something. Transparent means to essentially conceal things that are either too complex for the user or conceal them because we want the system to have degrees of freedom. So I want the system to figure out maybe where that binding is located. I don't want the program to hardwire an address into their code because it makes it inflexible. The system has no freedom to move things around,

maybe different machines and things like that. So this transparency is really a very important aspect of why RPCs are popular and why they kind of are the standard way of communicating today. day. But we talked about there are some issues that crop up that make certain types of transparency difficult. So one is failure. When we call our own procedure, unless we have a bug in it, it normally works until we get it bugged. But there are failure modes that are, in some sense, out of the client or the user's hands that could happen. Because we're communicating across a network. The network can be down at any particular point. The server machine can be down. It might be that the server is heavily used, heavily bogged down by other clients, and so it's extremely slow. And so to you, it kind of looks like it's failed. And so the question kind of arises, how much transparency do we want to try to have with respect to failure? And we call this failure semantics. And there's a couple of different choices generally. And normally the RPC system will either tell you this is what we supply, or might say you can choose which one you want, okay. So the simplest kind of semantic is what you would say is kind of best effort, which is we're not going to make any guarantees. And so, you know, in this particular case, so this is called maybe call, the caller can't tell if the remote procedure was executed or not. So it can't distinguish between whether the request was, you know, whether a machine was down, was the request lost, or the reply lost, or did, yeah, did it not execute at all, or did it execute but the reply never got back to me. It can't really tell. And this is the easiest thing to implement, obviously, if you're an implementer of the RPC system or implementer of the server. So either it will execute or it will not. And if it does not, an exception will be raised. But when the exception's raised, you don't really know, did the request go through and I just didn't get the answer back, or did the request not go through. So you're little bit of state of what do I do at this point. It's not working now.

So that's maybe called, it's the lightest kind of semantics. And so obviously what's a problem with this, if you're the client or the programmer? Yeah? You don't want your code to run twice? Yeah, you might, you're unsure, did it run or did it not run at the server, and so you might be tempted to run it again, call it again, you get a failure, comes back from the procedure call, and then I'll call it again. And what's the danger of calling it again? What could be a danger of calling it again? Yeah? Run it again, if it was like a bank transfer or something, you could send 200 bucks instead of 100. Yeah, if the operation you're doing at the server side changes the state of the server. And a good example that was just mentioned is adding money to a bank account or withdrawing from the bank account. So if it actually executed but you never get the answer back, you might say, okay, deposit again. And then you'll be adding more money and you'll be changing kind of the state of the world. It won't work the way you want it to work, okay? And unless you know something about the server, okay? But you might not know what to do. Or on the other hand, the request was lost and, you know, it never got, the deposit never got to the server. And so, yeah, you do want to execute it again. So you're in a bit of a quandary unless you know something about the semantics of what type of server you're speaking about. Can I just freely resend the command or resend the request? Is that going to be okay to do? We'll talk about that in a second. So another semantic that the RPC system can provide is, and we say client here, we mean the client stub, I should say, at least once. So the stub will continually try until it gets a response from the server. And in this particular case, a similar thing happens is there's no duplicate filtering. You might get the request executed multiple times because the client, the stub, will send out the request. It doesn't get the reply back in time. and it may send it again because it wants to guarantee sort of at least once, okay?

Here, what I was speaking of is the end user sees a client's sub-failure and then has to decide what to do. Similar problem here is the stub, the client and the stub might, you know, guarantee I'm going to keep trying until I get it happening at least once, okay? But at least once doesn't mean it didn't happen actually more than once, okay? So this adds a little bit of transparency in the sense that we're going to guarantee to the end user it executed at least once. This one, we're going to raise a failure to the end user. This one, we're going to try to hide the failure by retransmitting. And in this case, the similar problem occurs. You know, if the subclient just resends it and the server doesn't filter duplicates, it could add more money to your bank account. It could execute more than one time. So one solution when it mentions here duplicate filtering is the client resends it, but the server has some way of noting, did I receive that request already? So you're going to have to have some kind of encoding of the message, maybe with a special identifier, a special number. that would enable the server to see, oh, I received that already. It can't just check, did you deposit $200 earlier? Because maybe you are depositing it again. It has to say that this message is clearly one that has been already received. So you could do that. That would complicate the server a lot. The server would have to spend a lot of time always checking when it got a request, have I seen this already? So that would complicate the servers, and that's not something that people really like to do. But you could decide to do that, and so you could have at most once semantics. So this will guarantee that the server will not execute it more than one time, even if the client stub sends it repeatedly because of delays or because it thinks there's a failure or what have you. And here the server is willing to filter duplicates. And this is the most complex way to implement things and so forth. On the other hand, if the server has certain properties that we know about and maybe when you write a server you should think

about this, which is, can I write my service in such a way or my procedures in such a way that the effect of executing multiple times is the same as one time? Depositing money in a bank account is not such an example. I call deposit $200 three times, it's going to deposit $600. Right. But if I say, set my bank balance to be 400, set my bank balance to be 600, that is an example of a service that no matter how many times I execute set balance to be 400, like do the deposit locally, I can execute set balance to be 400 100 times. It's not going to change anything. So think of like an L, and the fancy word for that is idempotent. If you can write your server to do that, that would simplify some of the concerns about duplicates. So then I can really try to do something transparent and say it most once. So even if there's a, you know, the client's sending a new request, repeating it because it hasn't heard back, That could be happening while the older plus is coming back, and therefore the server, you know, could in theory do things more than one time, okay? But if you write the server in a way where it doesn't matter, that's really helpful. So I think about an elevator button as a service, if you will. if you say up one and that's what you're asking the elevator to do let's say by pressing the button a message is being sent to the elevator server up one is not an idempotent service or operation because if I send up one 15 times it's not the same as sending it one time it will go up 15 floors on the other hand if I say go to floor 17 if that's 15 floors away or whatever or one floor away I say go to 15, no matter how many times that is received in the server, it won't change anything, right? Go to 15, well, I'm on 15, okay, then fine, we're done. It won't create error conditions, right? It's inefficient maybe, but it doesn't make things break. It doesn't do bad things. So a lot of designers try to have services like that, okay? But there is obviously significant trade-offs, okay?

What do you think some trade-offs might be? I'm going to make all my services idempotent because I want failure to be really simplified because I don't want to have to do filtering and things like that. Suppose you try to write your service in such a way, assuming you can. What might be a trade-off? Yeah. You'd have to give the current state to the client. So if you have a bank account and you want it to be 200 instead of 100, you'd have to say that you have 100, and then they can add 100 on them instead of just saying add 100 to whatever's there. Yeah, it's pushing some of the state management over to the client. The client has to keep track of where they are and what the incremental thing they want to do is. and also makes things somewhat less efficient because normally the client has to send the state to the server, set my balance to be 400 as opposed to just increment it. Whatever it is, just increment it. And so it complicates the client a little bit. It has to send more information and it may be large information. So it could cost a little bit of efficiency but it makes the fault tolerance aspect or the transparency a little bit easier. And so for sure you would want to know, if you're a client or an end user even, what kind of semantics are being supplied. So that's an important dimension of this. Oops. Okay, and so another important area of RPC is performance, is optimization, because it's not a local, it looks like a local procedure, but it's going out to a remote server, and so it can be more expensive to do this, because of obviously the network, and there's a number of optimizations that people have come up with over the years to make RPCs run better. one simple case is an asynchronous RPC and so what we mean by that is don't block the client waiting for the answer to come back and of course the easy case in that is where there is no real return value and that's sometimes called a one-way RPC So if I'm telling the server just add a calendar entry to a calendar, an appointment for that day, I'm not expecting to get anything back necessarily.

So it would be nice if the system didn't sort of build in that synchronization and make me wait to when it actually completed because I might not be depending on that particular call. It's not returning a value necessarily. Okay. And that's sometimes called a one-way RPC. So, you know, there's maybe no reason to have my program sit there and wait for that to be completed. I say go off and add it, and now I go off and do something else. Okay. So that's one way. So at least don't block for an acknowledgement or anything like that. Just make it more asynchronous. And for a function that really doesn't return a value, that's a natural thing to want to do. The general case, though, there is a return value. Normally, you're asking for service, and normally you want something back in response. So you might say, a procedure might be, get today's calendar entry. Return something to me. Um, and, you know, in that case, at some point, you're going to have to synchronize and wait for that to come back, okay? But one interesting question is, you might not need this right away, okay? Yes, you need this value, it's returning something, but, you know, maybe you don't need it immediately. And should we have a way to sort of have that, you know, implemented? and let me show you, you know, that is something you might want and that's the general form of an asynchronous RPC so this is the synchronous form where you're just sort of waiting for the answer to come back and it could take a while before you move forward and this is like a regular procedure call and on the other hand you could break that synchrony up by using, for example, two underlying RPCs, one from the client to the server and then one back from the server to the client. So the client makes the request and just maybe waits for the request to be accepted or maybe for the network to have the message or whatever, and the client goes on and keeps going, does whatever, but the server hasn't done it, hasn't maybe be executed yet. Okay. Then when the server is finished executing the request, it could then execute an RPC back on the client to say, here's the results. Okay.

You asked for them a little bit ago. Now I'm calling back in to you to tell you I have the results. And that would somehow cause, you know, somehow the client is going to get interrupted and, you know, then be able to get the value. So this is obviously a fairly much more complicated mechanism under the hood. For one thing, it makes clients more complicated. They have to be able to not only initiate RPCs, but receive RPCs or some kind of message. but it can have it can lead to great performance benefits if you could do this so for example I'm making an RPC let's call it OP1 on a remote server and maybe it takes a long time to do this maybe it's a very expensive operation and in my program I might have a lot of code that maybe doesn't need A right away. So what I might like to have happen is A, so this operation is initiated, okay? And then my program continues. I continue to run things, okay? But the question is, and I understand that the server is going to return the value when it returns the value. That's great. So I know that's built in, this sort of asynchronous message or RPC coming back from the server. But the other piece that I need is there's a point in time in my program where I actually need that return value. So something like this, let's say. If I hit a statement that says, I want the value of A, I want to add one to it and maybe do something, assign it to a variable or print it or whatever, then you have to have a mechanism to recognize that, oh, okay, now the client has to block. Okay, so you did get some asynchrony in here, but you reach a point where the client actually needs a value. And so either you have to have a special statement you insert, or you have a smart compiler that can figure out that this is the point that you have to wait for the value. But again, it's a nice optimization because you can be doing lots of things in between while you're waiting for it. Okay. So that's sometimes called deferred blocking, and it works well with this kind of two

-way RPC, or, you know, two RPCs, in some sense. So that's an important potential optimization. optimization. Another optimization is sometimes called chaining or forwarding. So, you know, think about a regular procedure call mechanism. So if I'm calling a procedure and it has two arguments and one of them is the result of calling on another procedure, what normally happens is you execute this, wait for that to come back, build the request with those two parameters, then op1 will be called in a normal procedural mechanism. So one way to think about this is kind of this threat of control executing this is kind of waiting for the result of op2 to finish, calling a procedure, let's say. But in the case of an RPC, if these were both RPCs, then the main program doesn't necessarily need to have the value of op2 return to it. If you think about op1, this is an object, say op1 on this object. So to do OP1, you need B, and you need something else. And then you have your main program here. What you want to do, ideally, is instead of having your main program first have to determine the value of OP2, before it can call OP1, so this would be step 1, this would be step 2, You know, if this were a fairly large amount of data, then, you know, that's going to slow things down a bit. But suppose that just the purpose of this is to be a parameter to op1, okay? So, if that were the case, whatever object, let me say, this is, maybe op2 is on somebody else. step one, step two. If we could somehow tell op one, you know, here's a parameter B, and then why don't you call op two? So that the main doesn't need to get the intermediate result back. Op one calls over to op two, gets the value that it returns, and then uses that locally. And especially if these are actually the same object, object A and A, and this is object, say, B, give me different numbers. So object 1, object 1, object 2. So this supports operation 1, and this is supports operation two, if these were in fact the same object, then there would be, there would not be need for any communication across the network, okay? So this is called

forwarding, where the result of this is simply forwarded to where the outer call needs the value, okay? So it doesn't have to come back to the main program, and because these are all in the network, you know, that could add expense. And it may be, like I said, that, you know, the main program, as it continues, doesn't need this value itself. It just wants it forwarded to the other one, okay? And so that's an example of what's called forwarding or chaining, where we don't need OP2 to return a value to the main program. We just need that passed to the next chain, the next execution, which is OP1, okay? So questions about this, it's a little tricky, but basically it's like you're sort of, you're either telling, it's either what's happening is either op 1, you're sort of telling it, you go ahead and call op 2 directly. I don't need the main program to be getting the value back. Or, you tell OP2 to forward its output not back to the main program, but over to OP1. And if they're on the same machine, that's a trivial forwarding. So, this reduces the amount of data flow, potentially, across the network. Particularly, it reduces it to the calling program. Okay. So that's one optimization. Another one is something called lightweight RPC. And I won't say too much about it, but it turns out that a lot of RPCs are done between client and server that may be on the same machine. Okay. And in that case, and when the RPCs are very small, which they sometimes they can be, you can really optimize this particular case. And there's a number of lightweight RPCs or local RPCs that can do that, that can take advantage of that, the fact they're on the same machine. So I just want to point out that that does exist, and a lot of RPCs are done on the same machine, actually, surprisingly. Another thing you can do is called clumping. So if you're making multiple RPCs from the same client to the same server, maybe somehow we can put them together. At least maybe save, instead of sending two messages, maybe we can send one message with all the parameters as needed for both of the calls. So that's sometimes called clumping.

And a way to kind of see the different possibilities and why we might want optimization is to look at something like this example. So let's suppose you want to do an RPC to do image manipulation, okay? And so you have a stateless server that can do image operations, okay? And the image is very large. It's, say, a 1 million by 1 million set of pixels. And let's say our server has two straightforward operations. One is to simply scale it by some scale factor, so that's a scalar operation. and then a more complicated operation. And the result of scaling it is to return a new image, you know, updated image, and then a convolution, a convolve, which applies a filter to every point in the image, a more complicated operation, okay? Because it uses the neighbors to do the computing of updating. So, but it also returns, say, a new image, okay? And imagine the server is stateless. Stateless is very nice because that means the server can crash and come back, and it's fine. It's a more simple server, if you can do that. But suppose a client wants to make a call. It wants to first scale the matrix, scale the image by some scale factor S, and then it wants that matrix, or that image rather, to be then convolved by some filter F, let's say hypothetically. And what is the, if I do this as a standard normal RPC, what is the problem potentially with doing this? Yeah? Are you doing two RPCs, scale-in the control? I'm doing two RPCs, but furthermore, this image is for a huge amount of data. This RPC is happening first. All the data is coming back to the main program only to be sent back to that same server to do the convolution operation, right? And the reason is because the server's not really remembering anything, okay? And that's, you know, a problem or not a problem, but it can be extremely inefficient. And the question is, you know, if I'm designing, say, the server side, could I improve the situation for the client? And there are a number of possible solutions. One is forwarding. The mechanism I described a moment ago is, could I have somehow the output of this automatically get forwarded to the next operation at that server without having to come

way back to the main program and then ship back? And forwarding is really very handy when the operations are to the same object, the same server. So we could do forwarding. Another thing I could think about is clumping together, making the chain a big clump. I could say, well, if scaling and then convolving is a common thing, why don't I just make it one RPC? I could say convolve and scale, pass in the scale factor and the filter, and it would do them in that order. And this is handy because the input image is only flowing out one time, and the output's coming back one time. As opposed to flows once, it gets scaled, comes back, pass it again, it gets convolved, and comes back. But this is something you could do, but obviously it isn't necessarily the most elegant thing to do. Yeah? So in this case, what's the difference between clumping and forwarding if you forward in the same machine, but also if you get clumping, you have all your calls in the same? Clumping is basically collapsing the RPC into sort of a single RPC. And you can either do that, well, that's a good question. You can either do that explicitly by saying this is the interface, space, or you could sort of somehow detect that, oh, these are two RPCs going to the same machine. Could I do something clever? But even if you tried to do that, the server would have to be written in such a way to be able to handle the clump, right? It may not be written to do that. But this, you know, this is a little clunkier and a little, you know, messy. You know, I really want to have just convolvents and scale in separate things. So the ability to do forwarding would be a better solution. But it's much more complicated to get that to work, to get the output of something not going back to the origin and going to the next stage of where it needs to be. Another thing that you could do is change our server to be stateful. Let our server remember the state of the matrix. So then we might do something like this. We might say, okay, we're going to tell the server the image. Here's the image. Keep it,

because I'm about to do a bunch of stuff on it. And then I want to get the final answer back. And then you could do something of the form, you know, scale. Tell the server to scale it. It will scale it, okay? Nice and scaled. But no communication is happening. The image is staying updated in the server. You could say then convolve it. If this were to follow this, it would then convolve that image with the filter, again, changing the image at the server. And you can call hundreds of things, and they would all happen at the server without communication back to the client until the client wants to get the image. And so it could call get image. At the end of all these calls or if it wants to look at intermediate results, But here, the client only gets it when it actually needs it. So this is another approach that you could have, is allow the server to maintain the state of things. And write your operations of this form. Now, of course, the problem with this is that when you make things stateful like this, where the server remembers things, it makes idempotency much more difficult, okay? You know, if the system executes convolve twice, it's going to change the image. It's going to, you know, change it again, right? And that might need to be what you want if there's a failure. So when things are stateless, generally idempotency is a bit easier to sort of do, okay? Because if you think about it, you know, The client is passing the same image in the stateless case. Here's the input image, return me the scale value. If this RPC were to fail and this is more executed again, the client code is sending the same image again and scaling it. It works better. It's much more failure tolerant, but less efficient. So another beautiful trade -off that you can decide. Okay. Are there any questions about RPCs for the moment? So the optimizations is a very rich area people look at. It is, like I said, the de facto way, really things are, distributed systems are architected in terms of communication. It's just to kind of get into the semantics of what we mentioned earlier about hoarding versus clumping and chaining. Do we say hoarding
